title: Registration + Coffee & Tea at SRCCON
description: Get your badges and gear up for the first day of SRCCON! (Please grab breakfast on your own before you join us, but we'll also put out some awesome morning snacks a little later into our conference day.)

title: Welcome to SRCCON!
description: We'll kick things off with a welcome, some scene-setting, and some suggestions for getting the most out of SRCCON.

title: Reporting solutions when the world is on fire
description:
By most accounts, the media's current approach is neither creating a well-informed public nor truly holding the powerful to account. The industry's relentless focus on crisis and spectacle has driven away news consumers, fueled deep distrust in institutions and the media itself, and left many journalists burned out or leaving the profession altogether. Audiences are left overwhelmed, disengaged and without the information they need to address the biggest problems facing their communities. 

This workshop will create space for journalists to reflect on how to report on solutions at a time when many feel overwhelmed by the state of the world.  We'll explore how to cover responses without minimizing harm or injustice, and how to build trust with communities by working in partnership with them.  The goal is for participants to leave with actionable ideas, practical approaches and a renewed sense of possibility for reporting constructively toward a better future.

title: Uncovering the hidden ways that AI (money) is trying to shape AI coverage
description:
This session intends to start an honest and open conversation about an under-addressed issue in tech reporting—that the very AI stakeholders that we report on are increasingly trying to shape coverage—and discuss concerns, questions, and how journalists and newsrooms should respond. What are the costs of accepting funding or training, especially in a media landscape that is increasingly financially precarious? What questions should newsroom leaders and reporters be asking as we consider these opportunities? And ultimately, how can we best maintain journalistic norms and serve our readers amid the AI sector's increasing influence in both news coverage and our daily lives? 

We know this is a hard conversation for many, and we're not interested in ad hominem antagonizing of individuals or news outlets that are working with some of these organizations. Rather, we want to facilitate a conversation for people to share their own experiences, observations, concerns, and questions about emerging dynamics related to AI money and journalism—with the idea of coming away better-informed about the relationship between tech billionaires, AI boomers and doomers, newsrooms, and power.
facilitators: Klaudia Jaźwińska, Adam Becker, Eileen Guo, Shazeda Ahmed

title: How we cover (and survive) federal immigration operations as immigrant journalists
description:
When federal immigration actions unfold — whether it's a workplace raid, a federal-involved shooting, or protests in the streets — immigrant-led newsrooms are often covering the story while living it. In this session, journalists from Sahan Journal, a Minnesota newsroom reporting for immigrants by immigrants, will reflect on what they learned while reporting from the frontlines.

What does it mean to cover immigration enforcement when you or your loved ones have vulnerable immigration status? How do we prepare ourselves physically and mentally before stepping into uncertain situations? And how can newsroom leaders guide and protect their team under intense pressure? The panel will offer a candid look at what it means to report on immigration enforcement not as outsiders, but as members of the communities at stake.
facilitators: Cynthia Tu, Chao Xiong, Mohamed Ibrahim

title: People don't care about "local government." They care about their street.
description:
How do you do community reporting when your coverage area doesn't have a single geographical hub? Or when traditional public meetings are mostly performative? 

In this session, we'll talk about practical steps for finding and building relationships with people where they actually are—on specialized Facebook groups, block associations, nextdoor threads, or hanging out at local community spaces. We'll share methods for shifting your reporting lens away from top-down institutional updates and toward the lived experiences of individual residents. Bring your own examples of challenging coverage environments; we will collectively brainstorm actionable tactics to break out of bureaucratic reporting habits and focus on stories that connect directly to people's everyday concerns.

title: Think like an engineer: Building newsroom tools without knowing how to code
description:
Creating tools to automate or enable new workflows is tough, even with AI. It's not just about writing the code — translating ideas into a product that works takes time, effort, and planning beyond that. If you're starting from scratch, where do you start?

This hands-on session will walk participants through designing and building a web scraper with no prior coding experience, focusing on a process that starts with defining user requirements and planning a technical spec before any code is written at all. Participants will leave with a scraper of their own and foundational knowledge of how to approach coding with AI — along with resources to build on that foundation in their newsrooms.

title: News startup as laboratory: Transformative thinking and programming at AfroLA and Project Optimist
description:
Traditional public media configurations frequently struggle to evolve their core coverage models to meet the shifting needs of historically excluded groups. Startups, on the other hand, function like live product laboratories—unbound by legacy operational systems and uniquely free to explore non-traditional reporting rhythms.

This strategic overview highlights experimental workflows from AfroLA and Project Optimist. We'll explore how these organizations use solutions journalism, equity-centered tracking loops, and community workshops to build deep trust with readers. You will learn practical approaches for translating audience engagement metrics into editorial changes, managing editorial workflows, and designing civic-first media programs.

title: Social break
description: Take a break! Grab a snack, catch up with people you haven't seen in a while, say hi to someone new!

title: Still here: Why journalists stay in a profession in crisis
description:
Amid relentless mass corporate layoffs, industry-wide economic precarity, and severe institutional burnout, the prevailing public narrative surrounding journalism frequently focuses on why reporters leave. But what about the folks choosing to stay? What structures, support systems, and professional strategies allow journalists to continue doing deep public service reporting under immense industry-wide pressure?

This interactive panel gathers independent reporters and newsroom operators to talk through what sustains their dedication to the field. We will unpack concrete methods for protecting your psychological health, setting firm workplace boundaries, and discovering mutual support groups outside traditional management lines. This is a conversational session designed to share ideas and map practical blueprints for long-term career resilience.

title: Response, retrieval & resistance: Auditing how AI models handle politically contested information environments
description:
Generative Artificial Intelligence models are increasingly functioning as the primary gateways through which consumers search for political, medical, and civic information. Yet, the underlying mechanisms that govern how these models summarize complex, politically contested issues often remain hidden behind proprietary algorithms. When these systems hallucinate or prioritize unreliable sources during high-stakes public events, they introduce significant risks to democratic information spaces.

In this workshop, we will explore practical investigative methodologies for auditing automated chat interfaces and LLM search backends. We will demonstrate techniques for structured prompt testing, tracking drift in model behaviors, and cataloging automated bias when processing local political stories. Bring your own computers; you will learn how to design transparent audit trails that can inform accountability reporting on technology platforms.
facilitators: Madison Karas, Aaron Zou, David Kuszmar

title: Immigration inside the newsroom: How do we build better support networks for international journalists
description:
International journalists working on temporary visas encounter complex administrative hurdles, severe legal precarity, and structural employment dependencies that corporate newsrooms are frequently unequipped or unwilling to navigate. When visa regulations change or newsroom lay-offs occur, these reporters risk losing not just their jobs, but their legal status to remain in the country.

This collaborative session is a dedicated space for international media workers and news leaders to draft mutual-aid blueprints. We will audit common workplace vulnerabilities, share strategies for negotiating corporate sponsorship terms, and map out open-source legal and financial resource networks. We will work together to create an actionable list of best practices that editors can use to provide better institutional protections for foreign-born colleagues.

title: Where stories actually live: Understanding cultural memory and community storytelling
description:
Standard journalism training emphasizes fast, extractive reporting processes that treat community narratives like raw data to be pulled out on a deadline. This approach often ignores the long-standing oral histories, informal archives, and deep cultural memory that define neighborhood life. When reporters drop in without context, they frequently miss the structural dynamics of the stories they try to tell.

This strategic discussion reframes reporting through community archiving and cooperative storytelling frameworks. We will examine case studies of projects that partner directly with local preservationists, community elder councils, and artistic collectives to document local changes. You will explore practical techniques for slowing down your reporting cycles, tracking qualitative accuracy, and sharing narrative ownership with the communities you cover.

title: Make your data public (and usable)
description:
Too often in journalism, big data projects make a huge impact in communities across the country and world. And under the guise of competition, or just lack of resources, the raw data that could be useful for so many other things stays hidden on one person's computer.

Let's talk about how to get more of our data available to the public and in a useful format (i.e. downloadable data in smaller chunks for people who don't know how to use anything but Excel). And let's talk about making it a part of our permanent workflows.

title: Session coming soon!
Lunch at SRCCON
description: Let's eat together! Meals are provided when you attend SRCCON, and our local caterer will have tasty options for all dietary needs.

title: The biggest lessons we learned while covering science scams, fraud and art crime
description:
For nearly three years, I covered art and art crime for ARTnews. I didn't have a background in either beat, but successfully used my previous experience reporting on finance and economics to produce distinctive features, data and investigative coverage. This included reports on artworks and historical artifacts stolen from galleries, homes, and museums; museums repatriating stolen and looted items; art dealers accused of breaking US sanctions; NFT scams; the British Museum theft scandal; federal legislation about Nazi-looted artworks and unreturned Native ancestral remains; as well as a range of lawsuits about art loans, accusations of wrongful dismissals, copyright infringement, intellectual property, class-action cases against AI image generators, and high-profile art insurance claims. 

It turns out that art and art crime aren't as niche as many people think. This presentation will explain how I was able to find "local" art angles for national and international news stories, the tools I used and developed to cover these beats, and the simple secrets behind some of my biggest scoops.

title: You don't need to be an artist to do artistic journalism (and how not to use AI)
description:
Journalism is being flooded with perfectly structured, AI-generated material. But this isn't the first time a new technology has forced a medium to change. Photography pushed painting beyond realism. Radio made print dig deeper. New Journalism brought literary force into reportage. AI is the newest catalyst.

It should not push journalism to become more machine-like. It should push us toward what machines cannot produce: presence, risk, emotion, and form.

In this 75-minute interactive workshop, we will use artistic methods as working tools, not decoration. We'll look at examples from theater, street art, sound design, and exhibition-making, then break into groups for a rapid design sprint using participants' own upcoming stories.

No fine arts degree required. Participants will leave with practical prompts and a simple blueprint for transforming a standard pitch into an artistic journalism format they can test in their own newsroom.

title: Front-line leadership: How newsrooms manage risk, safety, and law enforcement coverage
description:
Directing an active news team through high-risk environments requires clear tactical operational workflows, rapid safety assessments, and strong boundaries against official surveillance structures. Too often, safety protocols remain abstract internal policies until an active assignment emergency develops on the ground, leaving editors and field reporters to make critical risk decisions under immense pressure.

This presentation offers a look into managing field risk portfolios, physical security logistics, and source tracking loops. We will deconstruct structural methods for building clear emergency communication check-ins, handling law enforcement interactions calmly, and building newsroom mutual aid loops. Participants will work through real-world scenarios to design actionable field safety guides for their own news organizations.
facilitators: Divya Tewari, Mia Warren, Meg Martin

title: Community Journalism — Lessons from your local farmers market
description:
What can journalism founders learn from neighborhood farmers markets? Both rely on building trust with residents, maintaining transparent production methods, and providing a regular, accessible gathering hub. When digital media companies get isolated from physical community spaces, their engagement patterns become extractive.

This active session explores non-traditional distribution footprints and community-centered business models. We will examine techniques for setting up mobile listening posts, hosting informal feedback pop-ups, and treating audience members like active civic partners rather than passive metrics. Bring a notebook; we will design lightweight community listening frameworks that can be deployed anywhere.

title: Understanding what gets tracked about your audience and their behavior in your product
description:
News product design is heavily driven by automated analytics dashboards, but many editorial teams don't fully understand the underlying data infrastructure tracking their readers. When we blindly rely on default telemetry scripts, we risk building intrusive tracking networks that can alienate audiences and conflict with basic journalistic ethics.

In this session, we will demystify product tracking tags, event listeners, and user identification pipelines. We will audit standard analytics packages, map out exactly what metrics are captured during a standard site visit, and discuss building privacy-first alternative dashboards. You will learn how to align product measurement strategies with a commitment to reader safety.

title: How do we create a world where non-writing journalists don't have to fight for co-bylines
description:
Despite the deeply collaborative nature of modern digital journalism, traditional newsroom credit structures continue to favor the text reporter above all others. Designers, software engineers, data analysts, and graphics producers frequently build the core infrastructure of an investigative project, yet they find themselves hidden in bottom credit footnotes or omitted from bylines entirely.

This workshop tackles equity in newsroom credit systems. We will review progressive, multi-disciplinary byline matrices that acknowledge different types of journalistic work. Participants will share real-world experiences navigating institutional pushback and build an actionable toolkit of credit templates designed to reshape labor recognition in your news organization.

title: Social break
description: Take a quick afternoon break to stretch your legs, grab some tea or coffee, and chat with fellow attendees.

title: Zines at SRCCON!
description: Join us for our first ever SRCCON Zinefest! We'll have tables set up with zines made by attendees, materials to start your own, and space to talk about the power of independent, analog print media in a digital world. Come browse, trade, and create together.

title: Dinner at SRCCON
description: Unwind after Day 1 with a delicious dinner on-site. Reconnect with colleagues, make new friends, and enjoy a relaxed evening together.

title: Session coming soon!
Session coming soon!
Session coming soon!
Session coming soon!
SRCCON opens
description: Doors open for Day 2! Grab morning drinks and catch up before we begin.

title: SRCCON morning welcome
description: Good morning! We'll run through quick house keeping items and announcements for our second day together.

title: Being kind to our emotional selves
description: We are emotional creatures first, and we often cover highly emotional topics. Yet newsrooms — and people in general — are usually not great at processing emotional moments (read: highly discomforted!). This session will help participants understand where emotions come from, broaden vocabulary around emotions, and provide a space to share ideas on how to recognize and move through these heightened sensations. Ultimately, developing these skills will lessen the chance of burnout.

title: How we investigated ethnic cleansing by the Sudanese Armed Forces
description:
Reporting on human rights abuses within active conflict zones requires handling difficult remote data pipelines, validating open-source satellite imagery, and protecting trauma-exposed sources. When official communication networks face government blockades, investigative teams must construct alternative verification networks.

This masterclass outlines the data pipelines and reporting workflows used to document human rights violations in Sudan. We will discuss methodologies for handling crowdsourced mobile footage, building secure translation loops, and verifying geolocation tags under high pressure. You will learn structural techniques for turning fragmented open-source records into high-impact accountability reporting.

title: Sound play and rebellious human-made craft
description:
As automated audio narration systems flood the digital ecosystem, standard news podcasts run the risk of becoming clinical and formulaic. True narrative engagement requires moving beyond standard production templates to explore the textured, hand-crafted potential of audio storytelling.

This active listening lab investigates unconventional sound engineering and multi-layered editing patterns. We will analyze audio pieces that integrate ambient textures, oral history loops, and non-linear spacing to communicate deep atmospheric context. You'll work through basic multi-track sound layouts designed to bring human character back into your audio journalism.

title: How to ask for help
description:
Newsroom hierarchies frequently frame asking for operational assistance as a structural weakness, forcing news nerds to resolve technical bottlenecks or reporting dead-ends in isolation. This dynamic stalls complex projects and contributes to profound staff turnover.

This session reframes internal communication by building practical transparency frameworks. We will dissect strategies for scoping technical hurdles clearly, communicating dependency delays to editorial stakeholders without friction, and building a newsroom culture that normalizes seeking help. We will run mock problem-solving drills to help you ask for help effectively.

title: Can local news work as a consumer product?
description:
When news organizations treat their digital output like click-driven commodity streams, they ignore standard user experience patterns that modern consumers expect from subscription apps. If we want readers to pay for journalism, we must treat our public service output like a responsive consumer product.

This presentation examines media organizations applying product-management frameworks to local reporting. We will analyze retention loops, onboarding funnels, and reader-centered utility layouts that go beyond standard chronological news feeds. Bring your current site layout challenges; we will map out tactical interface revisions designed to make local news indispensable.

title: Finding each other: A space for journalist-caregivers with young kids
description:
Balancing unpredictable breaking news cycles with child care commitments creates extreme scheduling strain that corporate media management frequently fails to acknowledge. Without institutional support or open discussions with peers, caregiving journalists often feel pushed out of competitive investigative tracks entirely.

This informal session provides an honest space for parents and caregivers to share adaptive workplace strategies. We will map out flexible scheduling patterns, discuss methods for managing leadership expectations, and build a supportive horizontal network of peers facing similar daily configurations. Come meet fellow parents and build community.

title: Social break
description: Take a breather, grab a snack, and chat with fellow attendees between sessions.

title: Let's talk about values
description:
As legacy business models collapse, media organizations frequently make strategic choices based on short-term financial opportunities rather than a clear set of civic principles. When internal editorial values aren't explicitly defined, it becomes difficult for product and editorial teams to row in the same direction.

This session is an interactive workshop designed to help you map and define your newsroom's core operating values. We will work through frameworks to align your audience outreach, data handling, and funding guidelines with a strong commitment to public service. You'll leave with a draft blueprint of core principles to share with your wider organization.

title: “I don't believe that this is encrypted so I think we're OK": Protecting your devices and your data from prying eyes
description:
Investigative reporters face broad digital surveillance risks that can compromise sensitive client logs, internal communication lines, and vulnerable sources. Standard commercial device configurations often contain hidden vulnerabilities that make data tracking simple for corporate or state tracking actors.

In this technical guide, we will build a complete operational defense workflow for personal devices. We will configure encrypted network relays, clear out unnecessary background telemetry loops, and review best practices for secure source intake systems. Bring your working computers; we will lock down your operating systems step-by-step to keep data safe from prying eyes.

title: Joy Breaks: Making space for joy in journalism
description:
The daily routine of tracking systemic political failures, community trauma, and economic challenges can quickly lead to deep emotional fatigue and newsroom cynicism. If our institutional cultures focus entirely on crisis management, we risk burning out the very reporters doing critical civic work.

This conversational session explores structural methods for integrating rest, creative play, and collective celebration directly into the newsroom environment. We will share case studies of organizations that use non-traditional meeting formats and collaborative project check-ins to support staff morale. Bring your own ideas; we will draft a practical toolkit for protecting joy in the workplace.

title: Designing tools journalists actually use
description:
Journalists complain about and build tools all the time, but are we designing them with real newsroom constraints or user interest in mind?

We build:
* Databases and dashboards that aren’t updated
* Databases of names of journalists. Does anyone use them?
* AI tools no one trusts
* Tools that are replications of what’s already been done

This interactive session will examine how tools get built and how do we get people to adopt them? When should we not build?

title: How to build a successful consumer-owned media co-op
description:
Relying strictly on venture capital or benevolent billionaire grants leaves local media organizations highly vulnerable to sudden shifts in investor priorities. Moving toward a consumer-owned cooperative business model shifts financial accountability directly to the community, establishing long-term revenue independence.

This strategic guide details the legal frameworks, governance matrices, and member management infrastructure needed to run a subscriber-owned media cooperative. We will discuss methods for hosting democratic board elections, managing equity pools, and communicating transparent operational budgets to member-owners. You'll leave with a practical blueprint for launching a community-owned media model.

title: Teach newsletters, not wire stories
description:
Standard journalism school curricula frequently continue to model their core reporting courses around legacy wire-service templates and inverted-pyramid text scripts. This framework leaves entering media workers unprepared for a media ecosystem driven by direct audience connection, niche newsletter formatting, and community products.

This educational workshop gathers instructors and newsroom trainers to update core teaching models. We will review curriculum modules centered on conversational writing styles, tracking newsletter engagement analytics, and managing direct subscriber communities. Participants will collaborate to assemble an open educational resources guide tailored for modern digital journalism classrooms.

title: Lunch at SRCCON
description: Enjoy a delicious lunch with your fellow attendees! Plenty of labeled options are provided for all dietary preferences.

title: Let's create a best practices guide for writing News Nerd documentation
description:
Internal newsroom software codebases, data scraper arrays, and graphics toolkits frequently lack clear developer documentation, turning critical digital infrastructure into vulnerable single-point-of-failure risks. When the primary engineer leaves a newsroom, their undocumented tools often break and get abandoned entirely.

This collaborative session is an active working space to outline a standardized guide for newsroom technical documentation. We will share lightweight frameworks for structuring README files, managing inline comments, and keeping deployment instructions clear for non-technical editorial staff. We will work together to create an open-source documentation template ready to deploy in your shop.

title: How we remade a secure whistleblowing system to support remote work and protect you from malware
description:
As editorial teams shift toward permanently distributed remote operations, maintaining secure leak submission networks introduces severe technical risks. Standard commercial dropboxes are vulnerable to tracking attacks, and remote workstations can easily compromise source data if they run into malware infections.

This presentation offers a look into rebuilding a secure, containerized whistleblowing drop system tailored for remote work. We will break down configurations for sandboxing untrusted file payloads, stripping metadata patterns, and isolating secure processing networks from standard corporate emails. You will learn practical patterns for maintaining high source defense standards outside a centralized newsroom perimeter.

title: Community skunk works (AI beyond capitalism)
description:
The current development trajectory of automated intelligence is heavily dominated by large, venture-backed tech companies focused strictly on commercial ad engagement models. If public interest media organizations simply accept these default systems, they inherit tracking frameworks that conflict with local community health.

This interactive sandbox showcases alternative, community-owned AI models and open-source data processors. We will explore how independent newsrooms can deploy small, localized language models that protect reader data privacy and prioritize local civic reference points. Bring an open mind; we will map out community-driven technology experiments that operate outside commercial systems.

title: From vibes to signals: Using imperfect data to shape newsletter strategy
description:
Small editorial operations frequently lack the dedicated data analytics engineering teams required to parse clean, multi-layered audience telemetry streams. Relying on rough email newsletter open rates or scattered subscription records can make strategic planning feel like a series of guesses based on raw vibes.

This presentation details how a small news team uses messy, incomplete telemetry data to make clear newsletter product adjustments. We will outline methodologies for cleaning up inconsistent click tracking logs, segmenting core reading habits, and building lightweight data loops without expensive dashboard systems. You will learn actionable habits for making confident editorial layout choices using the imperfect data you already track.

title: The future of news is accessible
description:
Complex interactives, advanced JavaScript maps, and asset-heavy investigative packages frequently omit critical digital accessibility standards, blocking disabled or low-vision audiences from reading public interest reporting. When accessibility is treated like a late-stage compliance step, the resulting layouts remain structurally flawed.

In this workshop, we will audit common frontend layouts and rebuild them around accessible design practices. We will discuss building screen-reader accessible tables, configuring responsive keyboard focus loops, and testing color ratios across dense data charts. Bring a link to a current project; we will work together to make your journalism accessible to everyone.

title: Print goes underground: Developing, designing, and distributing journalism zines
description:
When digital platforms fail communities — through paywalls, algorithmic suppression, language barriers or distrust — how can local journalists distribute reliable information? Zine makers have had an answer for decades: print it, fold it, put it in people's hands.

This session is a practitioner (and practitioner-curious) conversation about small-batch, alternative print as an emerging and legitimate tool for informing and engaging communities. We'll talk through our experiences and ideas for the three phases of zine production — develop, design, and distribute — using examples from recent journalism zines, student work, and entries from the SPJ's first-ever Zine Prize as jumping-off points. We'll start a shared best practices document together, and I'll try to turn our notes into a physical zine, printed overnight and in your hands on day two.

title: Social break
description: Afternoon coffee, tea, and snack break before our final session block of the conference.

title: Civic journalism, community organizing and how we build movement against the tide
description:
When news organizations limit their community role to objective news observation, they risk isolating themselves from the local mutual aid networks and grassroots civic efforts that keep neighborhoods resilient. True community accountability requires connecting public interest reporting directly to ongoing civic participation frameworks.

This strategic discussion investigates the intersection of investigative reporting and community organizing models. We will examine case studies of projects that work alongside local public libraries, community organizers, and neighborhood advocacy groups to distribute essential information. You'll learn frameworks for tracking real-world community solutions while protecting your core editorial independence.

title: Putting the Open back in Source: how can we make it as easy as 1-2-3, open source-ame?
description:
Publishing newsroom software tools under open-source software licenses doesn't automatically mean that external developers can easily deploy them. When installation files include undocumented system dependencies, confusing setup configurations, and complex database requirements, the code remains inaccessible to small newsrooms.

This presentation offers an actionable approach to building user-friendly open-source software. We will review practices for packaging repositories with container setups, writing clear onboarding files, and managing public code feedback loops without spending all your time in code maintenance. You'll learn how to build open-source tools that other newsrooms can deploy in a few simple steps.

title: Build a better budget: A collaborative Airtable session
description:
Managing complex multi-platform reporting grants, contract payroll distributions, and operational newsroom budgets across scattered spreadsheets frequently leads to data administrative errors and missed funding windows. Without centralized, relational tracking layouts, monitoring actual organizational expenses is slow and prone to friction.

In this technical guide, we will build a complete, integrated newsroom operational budget application inside Airtable. We will structure relational database schemas to track funding sources against specific project lines, configure dynamic forecast models, and review custom summary views designed for stakeholder updates. Bring your financial outlines; we will build a custom budgeting template ready to deploy.

title: Hiring doesn't have to suck for everyone
description:
This is meant to be a candid workshop and conversation about improving the hiring process from all sides. We'll highlight strategies that are working, like more clearly defined timelines, transparent communication between hiring teams and candidates, and rubrics to fairly evaluate candidates. And of course, NO GHOSTING (FROM BOTH SIDES!!!) 

We want to hear what's working in your shop to make hiring a little less painful. We'll also brainstorm strategies for making the case to newsroom leaders to change hiring processes, from baby steps to radical transformation.

Ideally this is also a space to help folks who are new to hiring or accidentally involved in hiring to widen their toolkit while also learning how to balance hiring duties with day-to-day jobs. Participants will walk away with a co-created list of small tweaks to major changes that have worked elsewhere to improve hiring, as well as a shared framework that can help build coalition around changes in the workplace.

title: SRCCON Closing
